quoteLast: reached the Bahr el Gazal; the junction has the appearance of a lake about three miles in length, by one in width, varying according to seasons. Although bank-full, there is no stream whatever from the Bahr el Gazal, and it has the appearance of a backwater formed by the Nile.
title: The Albert N'Yanza, Great Basin of the Nile, And Explorations of the Nile Sources
author: Baker, Samuel White, Sir, 1821-1893

quoteFirst: Having completed his schedule, the detective looked at his watch; it was
quoteLast: , a comparatively quiet time at clubs—and the same staff would probably be on duty as were there at the time of Wraile’s alibi for 24th October. Poole put on his hat and coat, walked out into Whitehall, flung himself on to a 53 bus as it gathered way past the Home Office, and was duly dropped as it swung past the Guards Memorial in Waterloo Place.
title: The Duke of York's steps

quoteFirst: In another place I saw over a factory which employed about 160 girls, who were worked from 5:30 a.m. to
quoteLast: with twenty minutes for each meal. If a girl "broke her contract" it was the custom to send her name to other factories so that she could not get work again.
title: The foundations of Japan
author: Robertson Scott, J. W. (John William), 1866-1962

quoteFirst: To this end, very kindly, _don't send me on newspapers_--I very particularly beseech you; it seems so to suggest that you imagine us living in privation of, or indifference to them: which is somehow such a sorry image. We are drenched with them and live up to our neck in them; _all_ the London morning ones by 8 a.m., and every scrap of an evening one by about
quoteLast: We see the former thus at exactly the same hour we should in town, and the last forms in which the latter appear very little more belatedly. They are not just now very exhilarating--but I can only take things in in waiting silence--bracing myself unutterably, and holding on somehow (though to God knows what!) in presence of perpetrations so gratuitously and infamously hideous as the destruction of Louvain and its accompaniments, for which I can't believe there won't be a tremendous day of reckoning.
title: The Letters of Henry James (Vol. II)
author: James, Henry, 1843-1916; Lubbock, Percy, 1879-1965 [Editor]

quoteFirst: Dr. Davenal and Mr. Cray went forth together. Outside the hall-door stood Julius Wild. It now wanted
quoteLast: . The Infirmary pupil had arrived a quarter of an hour before, and had waited patiently ever since to be let in. He had rung the bell in vain.
title: Oswald Cray: A Novel
